scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Plato's Philebus reflects the intellectual consensus and norms of the philosophical schools in 4th-century BCE Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Philebus is one of Plato's late dialogues, generally dated to around 350 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The dialogue describes the common Greek theological conceptions of the 4th-century BCE regarding the nature of the gods.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: As a late Platonic dialogue, the Philebus reflects philosophical developments near his floruit, circa 350 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The relationship between piety and divine favor describes a foundational religious norm in classical Greek society.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The work reflects contemporary 4th-century BCE religious and ethical attitudes in Athens.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This metaphysical claim about the 'true' perceptions of the pious is presented as a premise within the Socratic circles of Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The dialogue is situated in the mid-4th century BCE, a period covering Plato's late career.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Socrates utilizes general medical and psychological observations common to 4th-century BCE Athenian thought.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The observation is framed as a contemporary piece of wisdom in Plato's late dialogue, dated to circa 350 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The distinction between self-restraint and dissolution reflects the moral-educational hierarchies of the classical Greek polis.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the mid-4th century BCE, consistent with the composition of the Philebus.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The separation between 'the many' and those with knowledge reflects the elitist educational structure of classical Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the intellectual climate of the late Platonic Academy around 350 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This rule describes social perceptions of status and power in the 4th-century BCE Athenian context.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The psychological analysis of social status corresponds to Plato's late philosophical reflections circa 350 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The distinction between practical and philosophical arithmetic reflects the 4th-century BCE Athenian valuation of abstract knowledge over commerce.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to the composition of the late dialogues, roughly 350 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Technical hierarchies of the trades (techne) were a constant subject of classification in 4th-century BCE Athenian philosophy.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects late 4th-century BCE categorizations of science and handicraft.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The assessment of fields like medicine and generalship as 'imprecise' reflects 4th-century BCE debates on the nature of expertise.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Dated to approximately 350 BCE, reflecting late Platonic epistemology.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: This acknowledges the pragmatic necessity of 'imperfect' knowledge for artisans working in the physical environment of 4th-century BCE Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: A late Platonic concession to practical reality, written around 350 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The verbatim refers to a 'story' or cultural myth prevalent in Athens regarding divine leniency for lovers.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Philebus was composed near the end of Plato's life, around 350 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The exclusion of servants and entertainers from citizen discourse is a hallmark of the Athenian symposium.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: The Symposium is generally dated to the 380s BCE, reflecting social practices of the early 4th century.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The dismissal of the flute-girl to the 'women-folk within' illustrates the gendered spatial segregation of the 4th-century BCE Athenian home.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the norms of the mid-to-early 4th century BCE during Plato's middle period.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Social friction between the 'newly wealthy' (the money-bag friends) and the philosophical elite was a feature of 4th-century BCE Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects the social dynamics of the Athenian upper classes in the early 4th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: Pausanias explicitly mentions the compulsion to refrain from pursuing freeborn women as a social norm in Athens.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: As part of the andron culture described in the Symposium, this is dated to the work's period of composition around 385 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text specifically attributes this absolute license to 'our Athenian law' (nomos).
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Details the social and legal landscape of pederasty in Athens during the early 4th century BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The text refers to the 'vulgar opinion' (koine doxa) in Athens regarding divine indulgence for lovers' oaths.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Reflects mid-classical Athenian cultural beliefs about Eros and religion, circa 385 BCE.

scores.rule_polity_reasoning: The role of the paidagogos (tutor) in monitoring boys was a standard Athenian educational and social practice.
scores.rule_time_reasoning: Describes the traditional domestic and educational norms of 4th-century BCE Athens.
